<<no way out: the unheard story of meera>>

meera was born in the slums of mumbai, where the air was thick with the scent of waste and the sound of distant sirens. from a young age, she learned to read the signs of the world—when the rains would come, when the streetlights would flicker, and when the police would pass by with their boots on the ground. she was smart, but the system was not designed for her. every time she tried to climb higher, the walls closed in.

the adults in her life told her that the world was too big for one person, that the future was too uncertain for someone like her. they told her that she would never find a job, that she would never get a proper education, and that she would never be able to leave the slums. they told her that the only way out was to accept her fate, to let the system take care of her.

meera tried to be strong, to be brave, to be hopeful. but the system was not kind. it was not just about the challenges she faced—it was about the way the world treated her. the police came, the garbage trucks came, the street vendors came. they were not there to help her, they were there to make her feel small.

the system worked in ways she didn't understand. it gave her a job, but it also made her feel like she was just a number. it gave her a place to live, but it also made her feel like she was just a shadow in the corner of the world. it gave her a voice, but it also made her feel like she was just a whisper in the silence of the slums.

meera knew that the system was not just about the people in the slums—it was about the way the world treated them. it was about the way the system worked, the way it was built, and the way it was maintained. she knew that the only way out was to fight, to fight against the system, to fight against the world that made her feel small.

the system was not just a set of rules—it was a set of expectations, a set of beliefs, a set of truths. it was not just about the challenges she faced—it was about the way the world saw her, about the way the world treated her, and about the way the world would always see her.

meera was not just a girl in the slums—she was a symbol of the people who were trapped in
